text: - Say hi again to the people.
- All right, okay, hi camera, hi everybody.
I'm John Lindblad, I'm from WSocial.
And if you heard the previous session from Gander,
there's gonna be a lot of similarities here.
WSocial is also a new social medium being started up
in the same sort of context that we just heard.
And we are also very adamant about the ability
to be able to trust your feed
and maybe quiet things down a little bit.
So we sort of want to go back to what the golden age
when Twitter was new and actually useful,
where we had common understanding and global discussion
that we could really participate in in a useful way.
We are all for real humans,
quality journalism and open debate.
So why now?
Well, I don't need to go into this so much.
You, we already know the situation in the world
and in Europe, just as here in Canada,
there is a lot of strong feelings
about having something that's independent
and that nobody should be able to shut down.
But we don't want to be separatist,
just like most other people.
We want to be part of the whole ecosystem here,
but not being dependent on certain pieces here
that we don't control.
So I'll mention some core principles.
So we are hosted in Europe, both when it comes to ownership
and the actual hosting and all the services.
We verify humans, I'll come back to that.
We want to be open and transparent.
So show exactly what we are doing
and why we're doing things.
We want to allow free speech according
to European laws and norms.
We don't want to be interfering in what people say
and stuff.
Everybody should have their voice.
That's why we go back to these verified humans.
Anybody who is a human should have this democratic right
to say whatever they want.
Then as a participant in this network,
you also have your right to choose what to listen to
or not listen to, of course.
So democracy is very important to us.
We want to be good at Prado citizens.
If we, we are pretty new to this.
So if we goof, let us know.
We of course wanted to start on at Prado,
both from the sort of political point of view,
this is the right thing to do,
but this is also just as we noticed a cold start problem
without 40 million users would be difficult.
So it's a no brainer really.
Okay, verified humans.
You have already seen this.
This is a solved problem, right?
You just check that box and we know it's human.
Maybe.
No, so we really want to verify that your users are human.
Or we will allow bots and organizations
and things like this too,
but they will be marked as such.
This is an organization, this is a bot and so on.
So we actually go all the way to scanning passports
or ID documents.
So when you click on that little ID badge next to your name,
you will be able to see things about who you are.
You will see at least, right now,
it's only showing that you are a human,
but it will show more things about you
that you want to share.
You can also be anonymous here.
You don't have to share your real name,
but we will know that you are a human.
And how can we know that you're a human
and not knowing who you are?
Actually, I have this PDS call here.
You can see that when you get your profile,
you can tell that somebody is a human here.
Yeah, we are building the whole system up
into two separate domains.
We have one part of the system,
actually a separate company,
that is running something called Widentity.
And Widentity, that's the one that's scanning your passport
and storing locally in the phone who you are.
It will know your nationality,
birth date, full name.
In some countries,
it will know your social security number.
It will have the face of your photo,
photo of your face.
But it will not share that with WSocial.
So WSocial is a separate app,
separate company that knows, of course,
everything that you know about your online life,
your posts, your friends, your likes, and all that.
But they will not know who you are.
The only thing that unifies these two
is this UUID, 32 digit hex random number, basically.
So if you are on the WID side, identity side,
you will know that this is a human,
but not who it is on social media.
And on WSocial, you will know that this is somebody,
that's a real human, but not who that is.
We also bring over nationality and birth year,
or actually that's, we are planning to,
we are not doing that right now.
And optionally, somebody who really wants to verify,
I am really this person,
should have an ability to verify
that this is my real name, if they want to.
Some people might want to.
Who are we?
We are about two dozen people spread out over Europe.
WSocial is incorporated in Stockholm,
or actually it's not incorporated,
it's a foundation.
So it's a foundation that owns these two companies,
the W Identity Company and the WSocial Company.
We have a CEO, Anna Zeiter.
She has a background as privacy executive at eBay,
and she has a PhD in free speech and data,
GDPR and all those things.
Legal kind of background.
And I'm just a developer.
Business model, okay, so we will have some advertisements
at some point in the future, we think.
And having verified human users is a good thing,
because the advertisers typically want to pay more
for ads for humans than for ads for bots.
And we also, since we have this W Identity app
that really knows who you are,
and you can connect that app to wallets and banks and stuff,
it will be easy for us to add micropayments.
So that's really something that we're working on,
especially for news media.
So we want to work with the news medias.
We have negotiations ongoing with a number
of different European media houses
for how we should not steal their content,
but really provide the platform for their content.
So we want to redistribute, work together with media sites.
What did we do?
Okay, we launched this project in January in Davos.
And within one week of the announcement,
we noticed on our social media monitoring dashboards
that we had 1 billion social media views of posts
that mentioned W Social.
So some attention seems to have come our way.
Yeah, and we're just taking in the first few users this week.
We want to scale it up a little bit slowly
so let's see that our infrastructure holds up.
And the next steps here, so around May timeframe or so,
we think that we will be opening up for the waitlist.
We have a waitlist and then open up for anybody
without any sort of invitation codes around that time.
And what we hope to do with you here is of course,
we want to say hello.
We are glad to be here among at Prada friends.
And if anybody wants to come with some ideas, proposals, questions, or whatever it is,
just say hello.
Come to me and we'll talk.
This talk is coming to an end so it will have to be offline or afterwards.
We really want to be good at Prada citizens.
And since we're new to this, we're probably going to do a few things wrong.
I already, during this couple of days here, noticed a few things that I should have done differently.
We'll fix that whenever you see something that's a bit strange or perhaps even bad, let us know.
And we know that a lot of the things that we aspire to do on at Prado is already maybe happening somewhere,
like for example, there's permission data and stuff like that.
So let us know when we should be taking part in something like that.
And we also have a few ideas, especially around this, when it comes to account certification,
verification, and account types.
So this is a human account.
We want to have some sort of commonly agreed upon way to express that so that everybody in the atmosphere can tell.
Wish us luck.
Thank you.
